one_line_takeaway: QBugLM is a non-security, agentic benchmarking framework for LLM-based quantum-software bug detection/repair, whose case study shows that iterative retries (not elaborate prompting like CoT/ReAct) drive the largest accuracy gains, and that the open-source Qwen3 Coder Next matches Claude Sonnet 4.6's accuracy at 4-9x lower cost on most bug types except semantic deviations.
key_finding: Three headline results from the case study: (1) On the Bernstein-Vazirani circuit, structured prompting achieved the highest Pass@1 for both models (Claude Sonnet 4.6: 97%, Qwen3 Coder Next: 95%), outperforming Chain-of-Thought (90% and 45% respectively) and ReAct (95% and 63% respectively) -- contrary to the expectation that explicit reasoning scaffolds help reasoning-capable models. (2) Iterative retries matter far more than prompting strategy: without retries, Qwen3 scored only 20% on semantic deviation, deprecated syntax, and gate overuse, while Sonnet 4.6 scored 0% on those same three categories; both models reached 60% on structural error (single-shot). After two retries, Pass@5 reached 100% in all cases, though single-run Pass@1-style category scores after two retries left Sonnet 4.6 at only 80% on structural error and Qwen3 at only 92% on semantic deviation -- each model's one persistent weak spot. Overall, a single retry raised Pass@1 from below 25% to above 80% across both models. (3) Cost/efficiency: Qwen3 Coder Next was 4.8x, 9.1x, and 8.1x cheaper than Sonnet 4.6 on structural error, deprecated syntax, and gate overuse respectively ($0.042/$0.036/$0.061 vs. $0.202/$0.327/$0.496 per mutant) and 1.5-4.6x faster (e.g., 28s vs. 127.6s on structural error), while matching or beating Sonnet 4.6's Pass@1 on those categories. The exception was semantic deviation, where Qwen3 consumed ~350,000 tokens (vs. ~91,000 for Sonnet 4.6) and cost $0.198 vs. $0.369, yet still only reached 92% Pass@1 versus Sonnet 4.6's 100%.
core_contribution: The paper introduces QBugLM, a multi-agent benchmarking framework for evaluating LLM-based debugging of quantum software written in framework-agnostic OpenQASM 3.0. QBugLM consists of four components: (1) QBugGen, a mutation toolkit that injects a single bug per program from a four-category taxonomy (deprecated syntax, structural circuit errors, gate overuse/redundancy, and semantic deviation) into valid circuits drawn from MQT Bench, producing a ground-truth-annotated dataset; (2) QBugFind, an LLM agent that localizes and classifies the injected bug given the buggy source and a configurable prompting strategy; (3) QBugFix, a second LLM agent that repairs the program based on QBugFind's report, without constraints on the type of edit it may apply; and (4) QBugCheck, a deterministic validator that accepts a fix if the total variation distance between the measurement distributions of the repaired and reference programs (simulated noiselessly with Qiskit Aer, N=1,024 shots) is below a threshold epsilon_delta=0.05, and the transpiled gate-count difference is within epsilon_g=0. The authors also run a case study benchmarking Claude Sonnet 4.6 and Qwen3 Coder Next on five 5-qubit MQT Bench circuits (Deutsch-Jozsa, Grover, Bernstein-Vazirani, GHZ, W-state) across three prompting strategies (Structured, Chain-of-Thought, ReAct), using the unbiased Pass@k estimator and up to K=3 attempts (1 initial + 2 retries).
threat_model: Not applicable. This is a software-engineering benchmarking paper about automated bug injection, detection, and repair in quantum programs; it contains no adversarial threat model, attacker capability, or security property. The 'bugs' studied are unintentional software defects (syntax errors, structural circuit mistakes, redundant/incorrect gates, semantic deviations), not vulnerabilities, exploits, or malicious inputs. No claim of security relevance should be inferred.
limitations: The authors themselves identify two threats to validity: (1) class imbalance -- six mutation strategies produce semantic-deviation bugs, making the first three bug categories (C1-C3) highly sensitive to single failures at the per-category level; mitigated by reporting both per-category and aggregate results plus five independent runs with the Pass@k estimator. (2) Infrastructure failures (network timeouts, per-agent context-window exhaustion) are counted as unsuccessful fixes rather than excluded, which understates raw LLM capability but reflects realistic deployment conditions. Additional limitations evident from the text but not flagged as such by the authors: the case study uses only five 5-qubit circuits and two LLMs, single-fault injection only (one bug per program), and results are reported primarily for the Bernstein-Vazirani circuit in the main figures, limiting generalizability across circuit types and complexities -- acknowledged implicitly via the authors' stated future work to scale to larger circuits, more frameworks, multi-fault injection, and hybrid finder/fixer agent configurations.

datasets_used: MQT Bench (source of five 5-qubit OpenQASM 3.0 ground-truth circuits: Deutsch-Jozsa (dj), Grover (grover), Bernstein-Vazirani (bv), GHZ state (ghz), W state (wstate))
QBugGen-generated mutant dataset (bugs injected by the authors' own taxonomy-driven mutation toolkit; not a pre-existing external dataset)
quantitative_results: Pass@1 on Bernstein-Vazirani, structured prompting: Sonnet 4.6 = 97%, Qwen3 Coder Next = 95%
Pass@1 on Bernstein-Vazirani, CoT prompting: Sonnet 4.6 = 90%, Qwen3 Coder Next = 45%
Pass@1 on Bernstein-Vazirani, ReAct prompting: Sonnet 4.6 = 95%, Qwen3 Coder Next = 63%
Zero-retry Pass@1 per bug category: Qwen3 = 20% (semantic deviation, deprecated syntax, gate overuse), Sonnet 4.6 = 0% on those three; both = 60% on structural error
After two retries: Pass@5 = 100% in all bug categories for both models
Sonnet 4.6 after two retries: 100% on semantic deviation/deprecated syntax/gate overuse, 80% on structural error
Qwen3 after two retries: 100% on structural error/deprecated syntax/gate overuse, 92% on semantic deviation
A single retry raises Pass@1 from below 25% to above 80% (both models, aggregate)
Per-mutant cost: Qwen3 = $0.042 (structural error), $0.036 (deprecated syntax), $0.061 (gate overuse); Sonnet 4.6 = $0.202, $0.327, $0.496 respectively (4.8x, 9.1x, 8.1x more expensive)
Wall-clock time on structural error: Sonnet 4.6 = 127.6s average vs. Qwen3 = 28s average (Qwen3 1.5-4.6x faster across categories)
Semantic deviation token consumption: Qwen3 ~350,000 tokens vs. Sonnet 4.6 ~91,000 tokens; cost $0.198 (Qwen3) vs. $0.369 (Sonnet 4.6); Pass@1 92% (Qwen3) vs. 100% (Sonnet 4.6)

Experimental config: K=3 max attempts (1 initial + 2 retries), N=1,024 simulation shots, distribution tolerance epsilon_delta=0.05, gate-count tolerance epsilon_g=0, five independent runs per configuration
baselines_compared: Claude Sonnet 4.6 (Anthropic, proprietary) vs. Qwen3 Coder Next (Alibaba, open-source, code-specialized) as the two LLM agents benchmarked
Three prompting strategies compared against each other: Structured (general), Chain-of-Thought, and ReAct -- Structured is the paper's own best-performing configuration, not an external baseline
